text: The surgeon uses robotic assistance to dissect the hiatus and the angle of His. The esophagus is carefully separated from the right and left crura, allowing it to be encircled for adequate length before closing the hiatus.
The surgeon uses zero Ethibond pledgeted sutures to repair the hiatal hernia, placing three sutures to complete the repair. The large liver size is visible, affecting access during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a perigastric approach to define a retrogastric plane and create a small gastric pouch approximately five centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction. A stapler with reinforcement is employed to form the pouch, and a 34 French Ewald tube is inserted to calibrate it. Vertical stapler fires complete the formation of the lesser curve-based gastric pouch.
The surgeon divides the greater omentum to facilitate the performance of the gastroenterogenostomy, ensuring minimal tension during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz by reflecting the transverse mesocolon and constructs a 50-centimeter biliary limb. A gastrotomy and jejunotomy are performed, followed by the creation of a gastrojejunal anastomosis using a blue load stapler, calibrated to 30 millimeters. The Ewald tube is advanced, and a two-layer closure of the common enterotomy is completed using absorbable 2-0 V-lock sutures.
We used to perform this anastomosis with two ovicle suture. However, the V-lock has proven to be much more efficient, especially with the robotic system.

The surgeon converts the loop gastrojejunostomy to a Roux-en-Y configuration by dividing the afferent limb and creating a biliopancreatic limb on the patient's left side and a Roux limb on the right side. A white load robotic stapler is used to divide the limb. The Roux limb is measured to about one hundred centimeters distal, where a side-to-side anastomosis is created using the H-shaped technique. The stapler fires bidirectionally to form the H-shaped anastomosis, and the common enterotomy is closed transversely with a stapler.

The surgeon closes the mesentery defect using a permanent 2-0 Ethibond suture, ensuring no mesenteric hematoma is created. Peterson's defect is exposed by reflecting the Roux limb to the patient's left side and lifting the transverse mesocolon. The defect is then closed to the edge of the transverse colon.
The surgeon performs an endoscopy to visually inspect the newly created anastomosis, ensuring proper alignment and integrity of the connection.
